column1: How does the speed of a wave on a cord change if the wave amplitude is doubled?
column2: The wave speed remains constant because it depends only on the properties of the medium (tension and mass per unit length).

column1: What type of wave is produced in a rod when it is struck horizontally parallel to its length?
column2: It sets up primarily longitudinal waves because the particle displacement is parallel to the rod's length.

column1: Why does the speed of sound in a gas increase as the temperature rises?
column2: An increase in temperature causes a decrease in gas density, which leads to a higher speed of sound per the relation $v = \sqrt{\frac{B}{\rho}}$.

column1: According to the principle of superposition, what happens when two waves on a rope 'collide'?
column2: The waves pass through each other unaffected, and the total displacement at any point is the sum of the individual displacements.

column1: What is the mathematical relationship between the intensity ($I$) of a wave and its frequency ($f$)?
column2: The intensity is proportional to the square of the frequency ($I \propto f^{2}$).

column1: If the wavelength of a wave is halved while keeping the medium constant, what happens to its frequency?
column2: The frequency doubles, as the wave speed remains constant ($v = f \lambda$).

column1: How does shortening the string of a pendulum affect its period of oscillation?
column2: It decreases the period because the period is proportional to the square root of the string length ($T \propto \sqrt{L}$).

column1: How does the motion of a single point on a cord differ from the motion of the wave travelling along it?
column2: The wave travels at a constant speed, while the point on the cord moves perpendicularly with a speed that varies over time.

column1: Stretching a cord increases the tension and decreases the mass per unit length; how do these changes affect the wave speed?
column2: Both changes result in an increase in the speed of the wave on the cord ($v = \sqrt{\frac{F_{T}}{\mu}}$).

column1: What evidence exists to show that sound is a form of energy?
column2: Sound can do work, such as setting eardrums in motion or causing a glass goblet to shatter.

column1: Why is sound heard around corners or behind obstacles even when no straight-line path exists?
column2: Sound waves undergo diffraction, where they bend around the edges of obstacles.

column1: In a sound wave, what is the relationship between the displacement of air molecules and pressure differences?
column2: Sound waves can be characterised either by the longitudinal displacement of molecules or by the pressure differences that cause those displacements.

column1: What happens to the frequency of a sound wave as it passes from air into water?
column2: The frequency remains constant to prevent the interface from becoming an energy source or sink.

column1: As sound moves from air to water, how do its speed and wavelength change?
column2: Both the speed and the wavelength increase, while the frequency remains the same.

column1: What is the fundamental wavelength of an organ pipe of length $L$ that is open at both ends?
column2: The fundamental wavelength is $2L$.

column1: How does the fundamental frequency change when one end of an open organ pipe is closed?
column2: The fundamental frequency is cut in half because the fundamental wavelength doubles to $4L$.

column1: Why do speakers separated by a distance produce 'dead spots' in a room?
column2: Destructive interference occurs at locations where the path difference from the speakers is an odd integer multiple of half wavelengths.

column1: Two cars travel in the same direction at exactly the same speed; what is the Doppler shift of a horn heard between them?
column2: There is no Doppler shift because there is no relative motion between the source and the observer.

column1: How does the speed of a wave on a string compare to the speed of the sound it produces in the air?
column2: The two speeds are not necessarily the same, as they are determined by different physical properties of their respective media.

column1: The formula for the speed of a longitudinal wave in a solid rod depends on which two properties?
column2: It depends on the Elastic Modulus ($E$) and the density ($\rho$), given by $v = \sqrt{\frac{E}{\rho}}$.

column1: Define 'Beat Frequency'.
column2: It is the difference in frequency between two similar sound waves ($f_{beat} = |f_{1} - f_{2}|$).

column1: What is the range of sound frequencies audible to the average human ear?
column2: The audible range is approximately $20\text{ Hz}$ to $20,000\text{ Hz}$ ($20\text{ kHz}$).

column1: How is the amplitude of a wave related to the peak-to-peak distance of its oscillation?
column2: The amplitude is exactly half of the peak-to-peak distance.

column1: What is the condition for an object on the ground to lose contact during an earthquake wave?
column2: The maximum downward acceleration caused by the wave must be greater than the acceleration due to gravity ($g$).

column1: In the context of the Doppler effect, what happens to the observed frequency when an observer moves toward a stationary source?
column2: The observed frequency increases ($f' = f(1 + \frac{v_{obs}}{v_{snd}})$).

column1: If the intensity of a sound wave increases by a factor of $100$, by what factor does the displacement amplitude increase?
column2: The amplitude increases by a factor of $10$, as $A \propto \sqrt{I}$.

column1: What physical model is typically used to represent the resonance of the human ear canal?
column2: It is modeled as a closed pipe (closed at one end) with a length of approximately $2.5\text{ cm}$.

column1: In a pipe closed at one end, which harmonics are allowed to form standing waves?
column2: Only odd harmonics ($n = 1, 3, 5, \dots$) are allowed.

column1: What is the 'Mach number'?
column2: It is the ratio of the speed of an object to the speed of sound in the surrounding medium.

column1: Why is the frequency shift doubled in medical ultrasound applications measuring blood flow?
column2: The blood cells first act as a moving observer receiving the signal and then as a moving source reflecting it.

column1: How does the intensity of a spherical wave change with the distance ($r$) from the source?
column2: The intensity is inversely proportional to the square of the distance ($I \propto \frac{1}{r^{2}}$).

column1: What determines the wavelength of sound produced by a vibrating string?
column2: It is determined by the speed of sound in air and the frequency at which the string vibrates.

column1: Define 'Huygens' Principle'.
column2: Every point on a wavefront serves as a source of spherical secondary 'wavelets' that spread out in the forward direction at the speed of the wave.

column1: What is the main difference between mechanical waves (like sound) and electromagnetic waves regarding their travel?
column2: Mechanical waves require a medium to travel through, whereas electromagnetic waves can travel through a vacuum.

column1: In an EM wave, what is the orientation of the electric field, magnetic field, and direction of travel?
column2: All three are mutually perpendicular to each other.

column1: How does the speed of light ($c$) relate to frequency ($f$) and wavelength ($\lambda$)?
column2: The relationship is $c = f\lambda$.

column1: What happens to the energy transmitted by a wave if its amplitude is tripled?
column2: The energy transmitted increases by a factor of nine, as energy is proportional to the square of the amplitude ($E \propto A^{2}$).

column1: What is a 'node' in a standing wave pattern?
column2: A node is a point in the medium that remains stationary (zero displacement) due to destructive interference.

column1: What is an 'antinode' in a standing wave pattern?
column2: An antinode is a point in the medium that undergoes maximum displacement due to constructive interference.

column1: How does the wave speed in a string depend on its mass density ($\mu$)?
column2: The wave speed is inversely proportional to the square root of the mass per unit length ($v \propto \frac{1}{\sqrt{\mu}}$).

column1: What is the 'fundamental frequency' of a vibrating system?
column2: It is the lowest frequency of vibration that produces a standing wave pattern.

column1: How do 'overtones' relate to the fundamental frequency in a string fixed at both ends?
column2: Overtones are integer multiples of the fundamental frequency ($f_{n} = nf_{1}$).

column1: Under what condition does 'Total Internal Reflection' occur for sound waves moving from air to water?
column2: It occurs when the angle of incidence in air exceeds a critical angle such that the angle of refraction in water would be $90^{\circ}$.

column1: Why do low-frequency sounds diffract more than high-frequency sounds around large objects?
column2: Diffraction is more pronounced when the wavelength of the wave is comparable to or larger than the size of the obstacle.

column1: Formula: Wave speed in terms of period ($T$) and wavelength ($\lambda$)
column2: $v = \frac{\lambda}{T}$

column1: Concept: Ultrasound in medicine
column2: Definition: Sound waves with frequencies above $20,000\text{ Hz}$, used for non-invasive imaging of internal organs.

column1: In the Doppler effect, if a source moves *away* from a stationary observer, how does the observed frequency change?
column2: The observed frequency decreases ($f' = f \frac{v_{snd}}{v_{snd} + v_{src}}$).

column1: What is the relationship between wave intensity and the distance from the source for a line source (cylindrical spreading)?
column2: The intensity is inversely proportional to the distance ($I \propto \frac{1}{r}$).

column1: What does a 'ray' represent in wave optics and acoustics?
column2: A ray is a line drawn in the direction of wave propagation, perpendicular to the wavefronts.

column1: How many decibels (dB) correspond to a sound that is $100$ times more intense than the threshold of hearing?
column2: It corresponds to $20\text{ dB}$, as $\beta = 10 \log_{10}(\frac{I}{I_{0}})$.

column1: What happens to the displacement of a string at the moment two identical pulses of opposite sign perfectly overlap?
column2: The total displacement is zero, and all of the wave's energy is momentarily purely kinetic energy.

column1: Why is it easier to hear a truck before you see it when it is around a building corner?
column2: The long wavelengths of the low-frequency engine noise diffract around the corner more effectively than light waves.

column1: In a string fixed at both ends, how many nodes (including endpoints) are present in the second harmonic ($n=2$)?
column2: There are three nodes (two at the ends and one in the centre).

column1: How does the 'Bulk Modulus' ($B$) affect the speed of sound in a liquid?
column2: A higher Bulk Modulus (greater stiffness) increases the speed of sound ($v = \sqrt{\frac{B}{\rho}}$).

column1: What is the 'Threshold of Hearing' ($I_{0}$) value commonly used in decibel calculations?
If two sounds have levels of $60\text{ dB}$ and $70\text{ dB}$, what is the ratio of their intensities?
column2: The $70\text{ dB}$ sound is $10$ times more intense than the $60\text{ dB}$ sound.

column1: How is 'pitch' related to the physical properties of a sound wave?
column2: Pitch is the human perception of a sound wave's frequency.

column1: What determines the 'timbre' or quality of a musical note?
column2: The number and relative intensities of the various overtones (harmonics) present in the sound.

column1: The phenomenon of 'beats' is an example of interference in _____.
column2: Time

column1: What is the wavelength of the fundamental frequency in a pipe of length $L$ closed at one end?
column2: The fundamental wavelength is $4L$.

column1: In the DIKW paradigm mentioned for health professionals, what does the 'I' represent?
column2: Information

column1: How does increasing the frequency of a sound source affect the distance between its interference 'loud spots' in a room?
column2: It decreases the distance because higher frequency results in shorter wavelengths, placing the interference maxima closer together.

column1: What is the speed of sound in dry air at $20^{\circ}\text{C}$?
column2: Approximately $343\text{ m/s}$.

column1: How does the energy of a sound wave dissipate as it travels through water?
column2: Energy is lost due to the viscosity of the water, which leads to a decrease in the wave's amplitude.

column1: Why do musicians 'tune' their instruments to eliminate beats?
column2: Beats indicate a difference in frequency; eliminating them ensures the instruments are vibrating at the exact same frequency.

column1: Formula: Frequency ($f$) in terms of period ($T$)
column2: $f = \frac{1}{T}$

column1: What happens to the period of a pendulum if the mass of the bob is doubled?
column2: The period remains unchanged, as it does not depend on the mass of the bob.

column1: Identify the 'medium' for a wave travelling through a lake.
column2: The water in the lake.

column1: What defines a 'transverse' wave?
column2: A wave where the particles of the medium vibrate perpendicularly to the direction of wave travel.

column1: What defines a 'longitudinal' wave?
column2: A wave where the particles of the medium vibrate parallel to the direction of wave travel.
